scenario_title: Add CNAME and delete other data at same owner in one UPDATE

group_1.behavior: Processes the delete and add as a legal transition to a CNAME-only owner, returning success.
group_1.post_state: cnameX.campus.edu. has CNAME target.campus.edu. and no A rrset.

group_2.behavior: Rejects the UPDATE when it detects the CNAME exclusivity conflict against the starting state or as an intra-message violation.
group_2.post_state: cnameX.campus.edu. remains with A 192.0.2.30 and no CNAME.

expected_divergence.why_divergence: RFC 2136 §3.4.2 defines add/delete encoding but not the evaluation order for conflicting changes to the same owner in one UPDATE. RFC 1034 §3.6.2 and RFC 2181 §10.1 require that a CNAME owner have no other data, but they do not specify whether a server may treat a same-message delete of the other data as satisfying that rule before validating the CNAME add. As a result, implementations can reasonably differ between accepting the final CNAME-only state and rejecting the message with YXRRSET/SERVFAIL based on the pre-update state.
expected_divergence.test_rationale: This test starts with a simple A rrset at cnameX.campus.edu. and issues one UPDATE that both adds a CNAME and deletes the existing A rrset. That shape directly exercises the underspecified ordering between CNAME exclusivity checks and same-message deletes. A server that applies deletes first can end in a legal CNAME-only owner and answer NOERROR, while a server that validates the CNAME add against the pre-update state may reject the transaction. The follow-up CNAME and A queries make the final state observable and turn the ambiguity into a binary oracle.
